questions.text: Samples of the gases CH3Cl and Cl2 are mixed together and irradiated with light. Which compound is produced in trace amounts by a termination stage in the chain reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In one of the propagation steps, CH2Cl· is generated CH3Cl· → CH2Cl· +HCl It is possible for trace CH2ClCH2Cl to be formed as shown by the termination step: 2CH2Cl· → CH2ClCH2Cl

questions.explanation: In one of the propagation steps, CH2Cl· is generated CH3Cl· → CH2Cl· +HCl It is possible for trace CH2ClCH2Cl to be formed as shown by the termination step: 2CH2Cl· → CH2ClCH2Cl

questions.text: Which of the following alkenes is the most reactive towards the addition of bromine ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reactivity of alkenes towards the addition of bromine can be determined by the presence of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups, as well as the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during the addition reaction.

This is a simple alkene with no substituents. It does not have any electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups. The stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during bromine addition is relatively low, as there are no alkyl groups to stabilize the positive charge.

This alkene has two methyl groups attached to the second and third carbon atoms of the pentene chain. The presence of alkyl groups increases the stability of the intermediate carbocation, making it more reactive than a simple alkene.

The reactivity of alkenes towards the addition of bromine can be determined by the presence of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups, as well as the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during the addition reaction. This alkene has three methyl groups attached to the first and second carbon atoms of the propene chain. Like option C, the presence of alkyl groups enhances the stability of the intermediate carbocation, making it the most reactive alkene among the given options.

questions.explanation: The reactivity of alkenes towards the addition of bromine can be determined by the presence of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups, as well as the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during the addition reaction. This alkene has three methyl groups attached to the first and second carbon atoms of the propene chain. Like option C, the presence of alkyl groups enhances the stability of the intermediate carbocation, making it the most reactive alkene among the given options.

questions.text: In the presence of peroxide, hydrogen chloride and hydrogen iodide do not give anti-Markovnikov's addition to alkenes because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement isn't the primary reason for anti-Markovnikov addition. HCl and HI are indeed highly ionic compounds, meaning they consist of ions (H+ and Cl-/I-) due to the large electronegativity difference between hydrogen and chlorine/iodine. However, this ionic character alone doesn't dictate the regioselectivity of the reaction.

options.text: One is oxidising and the other is reducing
options.explanation: This statement is partially correct. Hydrogen iodide (HI) is a stronger reducing agent compared to hydrogen chloride (HCl). This difference in reducing power does play a role in the anti-Markovnikov addition. Peroxides (like H2O2) can generate free radicals, and in the presence of HI, the more reactive iodine radicals are formed. These iodine radicals abstract hydrogen from the less substituted carbon of the alkene, leading to anti-Markovnikov addition.

options.text: One of the step is endothermic in both the cases
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. The addition of HCl or HI to an alkene in the presence of peroxides involves a radical chain reaction mechanism. In these mechanisms, there are both exothermic (heat-releasing) and endothermic (heat-absorbing) steps. The key endothermic step is the initiation step, where peroxides are homolytically cleaved to form radicals. This step requires energy input, typically in the form of heat or light.

options.text: All the steps are exothermic in both the cases
options.explanation: This option is incorrect

questions.explanation: HI and HCl do not do this for energetic reasons. The addition of Cl and I radicals to alkanes in an anti-Markovnikov fashion is an endothermic reaction and therefore counterproductive. Without the presence of peroxide, all hydrogen halides will add according to the Markovnikov rule.

options.explanation: The ease of reaction is t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide. So tertiary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the fastest. Furthermore, A will undergro E2 elimination at a faster rate because Br -is a better leaving group than Cl-
The ease of reaction is t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide. So primary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the slowest.
While tertiary alkyl halides do undergo the reaction the fastest, Cl is not as good a leaving group as Br.
The ease of reaction is t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide. So primary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the slowest.

questions.text: Which of the following is the best method to prepare alkyl halides?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The reaction of alcohol with HCI in the presence of catalyst ZnCl2
options.explanation: This method is known as the Lucas reagent method and is used to convert alcohols into alkyl chlorides. However, it is not suitable for the preparation of alkyl bromides or alkyl iodides.

options.text: The reaction of alcohol with PCI5
options.explanation: This is a common method for converting alcohols into alkyl chlorides. PCl5 reacts with the alcohol to substitute the hydroxyl (-OH) group with a chlorine (-Cl) group. This method works well for primary and secondary alcohols.

options.text: The reaction of alcohol with PCI3
options.explanation: Similar to PCl5, this method can also be used to convert alcohols into alkyl chlorides. However, PCl3 is milder and typically used for converting primary alcohols to alkyl chlorides.

options.text: The reaction of alcohol with SOCl2 in the presence of a pyridine solvent
options.explanation: This method is commonly used to convert alcohols into alkyl chlorides and is known as Darzen's method. The reaction proceeds through an intermediate chlorosulfite ester. This method is generally applicable to primary and secondary alcohols and is the most suitable method.

questions.explanation: This method is commonly used to convert alcohols into alkyl chlorides and is known as Darzen's method. The reaction proceeds through an intermediate chlorosulfite ester. This method is generally applicable to primary and secondary alcohols and is the most suitable method. Therefore, option d is correct.
